text: We began by verifying the location of the tumor.

After taking down the inferior pulmonary ligament and lymph node dissection initially with level seven lymph nodes followed by level two through four lymph nodes, we turned our attention to the interlobar fissure between the middle and lower lobe.
A level 11 lymph node has been identified between the right upper lobe and the bronchus intermedius in the oblique fissure and dissected out.
Superficial to this node pocket is a safe passage from the interlobar fissure to the posterior hilum over the pulmonary artery.
Here, we divide the fissure with a stapler fired by the bedside assistant through the anterior port to identify the basilar artery.

The basilar segmental artery and bronchus are typically more accessible with an anterior thoracoscopic approach, since they are localized anterior relative to the superior segmental artery and bronchus.
Dissection of intraparenchymal lymph nodes during dissection of the bronchovascular structures will facilitate ligation and division of these structures.
The basilar trunk was identified in the interlobar fissure.
The middle lobe artery is identified proximal and anterior to the main basilar arterial trunk.
The superior segmental artery will arise proximal and posterior.
The basilar artery was dissected away from the bronchus by blind dissection, circumferentially controlled, and divided with a gold staple load through the anterior port.

To facilitate bronchial division, we proceeded with initial division of the basilar segmental vein from the inferior aspect of the hilum, which allowed to leave the lower lobe in the cephalad direction and identified the bronchus.
The key technical issue is to identify and preserve the superior segment of the vein during the dissection.
a gold staple load was introduced by the bedside assistant through the anterior port and the basilar vein was divided.
Following division of the vascular structures, our attention was turned to the lower lobe bronchus.
The lung was retracted posteriorly.
Once the basilar segmental bronchial branch had been exposed, it was circumferentially controlled and divided with two staple loads.
For division of intersegmental fissures we identified intersegmental planes using intraoperative fluorescence imaging following systemic administration of indocyanin green.
The demarcation line between segments was marked using electrocautery.
Once intersegmental fissures were identified, we took the lung parenchyma with multiple fires of the stapler.
At the end of the procedure, the lower lobe basilar segments were placed in the specimen retrieval bag and removed from the incision previously made at the 15mm assistant port site.
Following removal of the specimen, multilevel intercostal blocks were created and a single 28 French chest tube was placed through the camera port incision.
The lung was then inflated and the remaining incisions were closed.
